journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances

title-group.article-title: Educational interventions during the hospitalisation of patients with chronic respiratory disease: A systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aNursing area, Department of Medicine, Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, University of Oviedo, Spain
bHospital Universitario Central de Asturias, Oviedo, Spain
cSpanish Society of Pneumology and Thoracic Surgery (SEPAR), Barcelona, Spain
dGerencia de Asistencia Sanitaria de Zamora, Spain
eServicio de Neumología y Cirugía Torácica, Unidad de Investigación, Complejo Hospitalario Universitario Nuestra Señora de Candelaria, Santa Cruz de Tenerife, Spain
fFundación Canaria Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria de Canarias, Spain
gDeparment of Nusing, University of La Laguna, Tenerife, Spain
hHospital Universitario Marqués de Valdecilla, Santander, Spain
iInstituto de Investigación Sanitaria Marqués de Valdecilla (IDIVAL), Santander, Spain

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author at: C/ Julián Clavería s/n, 33006, Oviedo. sanchezzmarta@uniovi.es

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Background: Therapeutic education encompasses cognitive, psychomotor, emotional, and educational components, and is considered a fundamental tool in the management of chronic respiratory diseases. Its implementation during hospitalisation has been shown to have a positive impact on inhalation technique, patients’ perceived sense of control, and the reduction of inappropriate use of healthcare resources. However, health interventions involving therapeutic education are mainly integrated within primary care, with hospital-based activity often limited to episodes of exacerbation or acute illness.

Objective: The aim of this study was to synthesise the evidence on the effectiveness of educational interventions delivered during the hospital stay of adult patients with chronic respiratory disease.

Methods: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement, using the GRADE methodology to assess the quality of evidence for each outcome analysed, as well as the Revised Cochrane risk-of-bias tool for randomised trials to evaluate the methodological quality and risk of bias.

Results: Eleven studies, comprising a total of 1,925 participants, were included. Interventions encompassed educational programmes, motivational interviews, physical training plans, specific inhalation technique training, video interventions, and mobile apps for self-management. Thirteen clinical and patient-reported outcomes were analysed, with contradictory findings; consistently positive effects were observed only for quality of life. According to the GRADE methodology, all outcomes were supported by low or very low quality of evidence due to methodological limitations of the included studies, including risk of bias related to lack of blinding in educational interventions, small sample sizes, heterogeneity in intervention design and outcome measurement, and imprecision of effect estimates.

Conclusion: Educational interventions conducted during hospitalisation for patients with chronic respiratory disease are limited and demonstrate a low or very low quality of evidence for all evaluated outcomes. It is imperative to design research studies that minimise the risks of bias and identify activities that can improve outcomes.
Registration: PROSPERO identifier number CRD42022358094; https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/ registered 19/09/2022.

boxed-text.p: • Chronic respiratory diseases are associated with high morbidity and mortality rates, as well as frequent unplanned medical care.
• Therapeutic education plays a pivotal role in maintaining and enhancing quality of life, promoting treatment compliance, and reducing exacerbations and unnecessary hospital admissions.
• Health interventions are primarily integrated into primary care, relegating hospital activity to situations of exacerbation or acute episodes.
• Educational interventions carried out during the hospitalisation of patients with chronic respiratory disease are limited and show low and very low quality of evidence effects.
• it is essential to implement specific interventions during hospitalisation, involving coordinated participation from multidisciplinary team professionals.

notes.p: Alt-text: Unlabelled box dummy alt text

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Chronic respiratory diseases (CRDs) are conditions affecting the lungs and/or airways that lack a definitive curative treatment and generally progress slowly, impacting hundreds of millions of people worldwide (Wang et al., 2025; Yuan et al., 2025). The most prevalent CRDs are asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and they represent a significant burden on healthcare systems due to their high morbidity and mortality rates, as well as the frequent need for unplanned medical care (World Health Organization, 2024a, 2024b, 2025). Beyond pharmacological treatment, current clinical management of these conditions emphasises the central role of self-care, understood as the set of skills that enable patients to manage their illness actively and on an ongoing basis (Cosío et al., 2022; Vaquero-Lozano et al., 2021)

According to the World Health Organisation, therapeutic education (TE) is a key element in training patients in self-care by specifically trained professionals, aiming to maintain and improve quality of life (World Health Organization, 2023). It also seeks to enhance disease-related knowledge, promote treatment adherence, empower patients to correctly administer their treatment, strengthen self-efficacy, and reduce avoidable exacerbations and hospital admissions (Press et al., 2020, 2017; Trela et al., 2022; Vázquez-González et al., 2023).

The key components of TE are structured around several domains: the cognitive domain addresses knowledge about the condition, its triggers, and treatment; the psychomotor domain involves the training of practical skills; the behavioural domain includes the development and use of personalised action plans; the emotional domain focuses on strengthening the patient’s confidence in their ability to manage their condition (self-efficacy); and the educational domain employs active, adaptive, and multimodal methodologies to facilitate learning and promote long-term knowledge retention (Press et al., 2017; Trela et al., 2022; Vázquez-González et al., 2023).

International scientific societies and health systems worldwide agree that TE should encompass a range of educational interventions focused on developing self-care competencies, such as increasing knowledge about the disease, ensuring the correct use of inhalation devices, identifying warning signs, and implementing individualised action plans (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), 2018; Spanish Society of Pneumology and Thoracic Surgery, 2022). In patients with cystic fibrosis, the development of self-care skills is essential—both during the transition from paediatric to adult care and throughout inpatient follow-up. Key competencies identified as fundamental to promoting continuity of care and clinical stability include acquiring knowledge of the disease, administering treatments correctly, adhering to respiratory physiotherapy regimens, managing healthcare appointments independently, and developing autonomy in symptom management (Coca Barbado et al., 2014). In addition, the emotional state of patients—particularly during hospitalisation or other critical phases such as the transition from child to adult services—has a significant impact on treatment adherence and overall quality of life (Coca Barbado et al., 2014). In patients with lung cancer, educational interventions grounded in theories of emotional adaptation and health education have proven effective in enhancing self-care during hospital stays. These interventions have been shown to reduce levels of anxiety and depression, improve respiratory condition, and increase patient satisfaction with the care received (Trela et al., 2022).

Although interventions can be delivered through various formats—such as individual and group sessions, audiovisual support, verbal information, written materials, telephone reinforcement, email communication, in-person consultations, hospital ward interactions, and home visits—the content provided also determines their clinical impact (Lopez-Campos et al., 2022; National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), 2018; Spanish Society of Pneumology and Thoracic Surgery, 2022). However, the available evidence shows considerable variability in the outcomes reported, a point that has been widely acknowledged in the literature (Kristjansdottir et al., 2021). This disparity can be attributed both to differences in the educational content delivered and to the methodological heterogeneity of the interventions—encompassing factors such as the mode of delivery, programme intensity and duration, the professional profile of the educator, the level of care at which the intervention is implemented, and the pedagogical resources employed (Aboumatar et al., 2019; Barker et al., 2020; Kristjansdottir et al., 2021; Press et al., 2020). Clearly distinguishing between the educational content and the structural components of the intervention is essential for accurately interpreting its effectiveness.

Various approaches have been developed to structure educational interventions targeting patients with CRDs, and their classification typically considers the methods used, the intensity of the intervention, and the level of care in which they are implemented.

One of the major challenges of TE lies in the setting or level of care at which it is delivered, as this significantly influences its design, mode of implementation, intensity, and outcomes. In hospital settings, patients are typically in a critical phase of their illness, which may enhance their receptiveness to learning and allow for intensive interventions focused on practical aspects such as correcting technical errors and optimising inhaler use (Press et al., 2020, 2017). Therefore, the care setting not only affects the delivery mode and educational ‘dose’, but also determines the type of professionals involved, the resources available, and the degree of personalisation achievable—all of which directly influence the effectiveness of TE. Despite this, health interventions employing TE and longitudinal follow-up are still primarily integrated into primary care, relegating hospital activity to situations of exacerbation or acute episodes where symptom treatment and patient stabilisation are the primary focus (Collinsworth et al., 2018).

The objective of this study was to synthesise the available evidence on the effectiveness of educational interventions conducted during the hospitalisation of patients with chronic respiratory disease.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review is registered on PROSPERO under the identifier number CRD42022358094 (Sánchez-Zaballos et al., 2022) and has been reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement (Page et al., 2021).

sec.title: Research question (PICO format)
sec.p: In hospitalised adult patients with chronic respiratory disease (P), do structured educational interventions delivered during hospitalisation (I), compared to usual care (C), lead to improved clinical and patient-reported outcomes (O)?

sec.title: Participants, interventions, comparison and outcomes
sec.p: Inclusion criteria: Hospitalised patients with chronic respiratory disease (asthma, COPD, lung cancer, cystic fibrosis) who have undergone a specific educational intervention designed to reduce exacerbations and/or hospital readmissions, manage symptoms, reduce mortality, improve quality of life, provide inhalation technique training, and/or enhance knowledge. These interventions were compared against a control group receiving usual clinical practice by an unspecified healthcare professional, with follow-up after hospital discharge.

Exclusion criteria: Interventions conducted in emergency departments or rehabilitation centres/clinics; those not performed during hospitalisation, and those not directly involving the patient (e.g. interventions involving only parents and children).

sec.title: Types of studies included
sec.p: Clinical trials, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses published between 2012 and 2025, written in Spanish, English, French, and/or Portuguese. Systematic reviews identified during screening were not included as units of analysis. They were used exclusively as a supplementary source to identify potentially eligible primary trials that might not have been retrieved through database searches. Only primary clinical trials meeting the inclusion criteria were included in the final analysis.

sec.title: Data sources
sec.p: The search was conducted in the following databases: Medline via PubMed, CINAHL, EMBASE, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, Cochrane Library, CUIDEN Plus, Virtual Health Library, Epistemonikos, and by reference searching.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: The search was conducted on February 15, 2022, and subsequently updated on January 10, 2026, by a team of four researchers. Each member was randomly assigned a specific data source. The search terms included the following Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) terms: Patient Education as Topic; Respiratory Tract Diseases; Hospitalization; Chronic Disease; Self-management; and free terms such as Therapeutic Education; Asthma; COPD; Lung Cancer; Cystic Fibrosis; Ward; and Inpatient. The complete Boolean search strings for each database, along with the limits applied, are provided in supplementary material.

After compiling the list of publications, duplicates were removed. Subsequently, two independent rounds of peer review of the titles and abstracts were conducted to eliminate manuscripts that did not meet the inclusion criteria. In cases of disagreement regarding the suitability of a publication, a third review was performed.

The publications that passed this initial screening underwent a further round of peer review, involving a comprehensive full-text assessment to ensure full compliance with the inclusion and exclusion criteria.

Following this, a critical appraisal was conducted using the CASPe network tools (CASPe, 2022). A predefined methodological quality threshold was applied to prioritise internal validity, and only publications scoring 7 out of 10 or higher were included in the subsequent phase. This criterion was established to ensure that conclusions were supported by trials meeting minimum methodological standards.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data extraction (supplementary material) was conducted using the OSTEBA Critical Reading Sheets through their software application (Osteba, 2024): cite, study (design, objectives, settings, professionals who develop the intervention), PICO question, methods (participants per group, intervention in experimental and control group, masking and randomization, post-randomization losses, follow-up), results (positive and adverse effects, limitations) and conclusions.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: A peer review was performed using the Revised Cochrane risk-of-bias tool for randomised trials (RoB 2.0) to analyse the methodological quality and risk of bias (Cochrane Methods Bias, 2024).

sec.title: Summary of findings and assessment of the certainty of the evidence
sec.p:
The GRADE methodology was employed to evaluate the quality of evidence for each outcome analysed (Guyatt et al., 2008). Four researchers independently conducted the assessment using the GRADEpro GDT tool. The certainty of evidence was classified as high, moderate, low, or very low based on five domains: risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and publication bias (GRADEpro, 2024). Discrepancies were resolved by consensus. Summary of Findings table were developed for the outcomes assessed (Table 1).Table 1
Summary findings and quality of evidence according to the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) system.Table 1 dummy alt text
Certainty assessment№ of patientsEffectQuality of evidence (GRADE)*
Outcomes№ of studiesStudy DesignRisk of biasInconsistencyIndirectnessImprecisionOther considerationsEducational intervertionTraditional educationRelative (95 % CI)Absolute (95 % CI)
Knowledge about the disease2Randomized trialsVery seriousVery seriousVery seriousNot seriousPublication bias strongly suspected18/35 (51.4 %)17/35 (48.6 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Exacerbations9Randomized trialsSeriousSeriousNot seriousSeriousNone889/1782 (49.9 %)893/1782 (50.1 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Physical capacity2Randomized trialsNot seriousSeriousNot seriousSeriousPublication bias strongly suspected218/411 (53.0 %)215/411 (52.3 %)Not estimable⨁⨁◯◯ Low
Quality of life4Randomized trialsSeriousSeriousSeriousExtremely seriousNone251/508 (59.4 %)248/508 (47.6 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Smoking habit1Randomized trialsSeriousVery seriousVery seriousExtremely seriousPublication bias strongly suspected35/78 (44.9 %)33/78 (42.3 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Exercise/physical activity2Randomized trialsSeriousSeriousSeriousSeriousNone143/292 (49.0 %)139/292 (47.6 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Inhalation technique3Randomized trialsSeriousNot seriousNot seriousSeriousNone145/288 (50.3 %)143/288 (49.7 %)no estimable⨁⨁◯◯ Low
Adherence to inhaled therapy2Randomized trialsNot seriousSeriousVery seriousSeriousNone80/155 (51.6 %)75/155 (48.4 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Mortality3Randomized trialsNot seriousSeriousSeriousSeriousNone534/1072 (49.8 %)538/1072 (50.2 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Skills and self-management5Randomized trialsSeriousNot seriousNot seriousSeriousNone411/824 (49.9 %)413/824 (50.1 %)Not estimable⨁⨁◯◯ Low
Attitudes towards the disease3Randomized trialsSeriousVery seriousVery seriousExtremely seriousPublication bias strongly suspected166/340 (48.8 %)174/340 (51.2 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Adherence to pharmacological treatment1Randomized trialsSeriousSeriousVery seriousVery seriousNone18/35 (51.4 %)17/35 (48.6 %)Not estimable⨁◯◯◯ Very low
Length of hospital stay3Randomized trialsNot seriousSeriousNot seriousSeriousNone469/939 (50.2 %)493/989 (49.8 %)Not estimable⨁⨁◯◯ Low
*High: we are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect.
Moderate: we are moderately confident in the effect estimate: The true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different.
Low: the confidence in the effect estimate is limited. The true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effect.
Very low: we have very little confidence in the effect estimate: The true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
The search strategies initially identified 368 articles. After removing duplicates, 207 articles were excluded based on title and abstract, with a third reviewer required in 34 cases. Ultimately, eleven documents were included in the systematic review (Benzo et al., 2016; Greening et al., 2014; Ho et al., 2025; Lara Romero et al., 2018; Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Lopez-Lopez et al., 2020; Press et al., 2020, 2016, 2012; Silver et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021) (Figure 1). The main characteristics of the studies, as well as their interventions and outcomes are detailed in the supplementary material.Fig. 1
PRISMA Flowchart.Fig 1 dummy alt text

sec.title: Characteristics of the studies
sec.p: All studies were randomized clinical trials conducted in various countries: six in the United States (Benzo et al., 2016; Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Press et al., 2020, 2016, 2012; Silver et al., 2017), one in the United Kingdom (Greening et al., 2014), two in China (Ho et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2021), and two in Spain (Lara Romero et al., 2018; Lopez-Lopez et al., 2020).

sec.title: Characteristics of the participants
sec.p: A total of 1,925 participants were included, with sample sizes ranging from 35 to 428 participants and ages between 35 and 79 years. Sex distribution was equal in all studies except for six, which reported a higher participation of women in three instances (Press et al., 2020, 2016, 2012) and men in two (Lara Romero et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021), with one study including exclusively male participant (Ho et al., 2025). Race or ethnic background was recorded in four studies, with a higher participation of African American individuals in three clinical trials (Press et al., 2020, 2016, 2012) (up to 90 % of the sample) and a similar distribution between African Americans and Caucasians in the other study (Silver et al., 2017).

sec.title: Interventions
sec.p: Various types of interventions were implemented, with no two designs being identical. Eight studies involved educational programmes (Benzo et al., 2016; Greening et al., 2014; Ho et al., 2025; Lara Romero et al., 2018; Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Lopez-Lopez et al., 2020; Silver et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021), with two of these adding motivational interviews (Benzo et al., 2016; Linden and Butterworth, 2014), and two combining this with a specific physical training plan as the main intervention (Greening et al., 2014; Lopez-Lopez et al., 2020). Two studies focused on specific inhalation technique training (Press et al., 2020, 2016), one used video intervention (Press et al., 2012), and another utilised a mobile app for self-management (Wang et al., 2021). The content of the educational programmes is described in detail in the supplementary material.

The professional profiles of those conducting the interventions varied across the studies: in three trials (published by the same research team), non-clinical staff specifically trained for the purpose conducted the interventions (United States); in two studies, respiratory therapists conducted the interventions (United States); in four cases, nurses conducted them (China and Spain); and in one study, both nurses and physiotherapists were involved (United Kingdom).

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: The analysed outcomes encompassed clinical, behavioural, and patient-reported domains. Clinical outcomes included exacerbations, mortality, and length of hospital stay, while behavioural and treatment-related outcomes comprised inhalation technique, adherence to inhaled and pharmacological therapy, smoking status, exercise or physical activity, physical capacity, and disease-related knowledge. Patient-reported outcomes focused on quality of life, attitudes toward the disease, and self-management skills.

sec.title: Risks of bias
sec.p:
The primary risks of bias were related to the randomisation process and selective reporting of results, both identified in seven of the studies (Figure 2). This risk-of-bias profile directly informed the GRADE certainty ratings, as concerns regarding randomisation procedures and the lack of blinding in most trials contributed to the downgrading of evidence certainty across outcomes.Fig. 2
Risk of bias according to RoB 2.0.Fig 2 dummy alt text

sec.title: Exacerbations
sec.p: Nine studies analysed exacerbations measured through hospital readmissions (Benzo et al., 2016; Greening et al., 2014; Ho et al., 2025; Lara Romero et al., 2018; Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Press et al., 2020, 2016, 2012; Silver et al., 2017). Only two studies considered the reason for readmission due to respiratory disease (COPD) (Benzo et al., 2016; Silver et al., 2017), while the rest included readmissions regardless of the cause. The follow-up period for the intervention effects varied across the studies: 1 month (Lara Romero et al., 2018; Press et al., 2020, 2012); 3 months (Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Press et al., 2016); 6 months (Silver et al., 2017); and 12 months (Benzo et al., 2016; Greening et al., 2014).

The results were positive in five studies; however, two of these only conducted a bivariate analysis (Press et al., 2016, 2012), meaning that their conclusions should not be considered reliable for this variable. The publication by Benzo et al. (2016) confirmed a reduction in COPD exacerbations at 1 month (OR 0.092, 95 % CI 0.011 to 0.769, p = 0.0276), 3 months (OR 0.371, 95 % CI 0.150 to 0.916, p = 0.0315), and 6 months (OR 0.430, 95 % CI 0.196 to 0.940, p = 0.0344), but not at 12 months. The intervention consisted of a multicomponent educational programme that included motivational interviewing. Similarly, Silver et al. (2017) confirmed the positive effect of their intervention at 6 months (beta coefficient -0.714; 95 % CI -1.056 to -0.372, p < 0.001.

Despite the positive effects observed in the short term, the quality of the evidence was rated as very low using the GRADE system due to risk of bias related to the lack of blinding in educational interventions, heterogeneity in intervention design and outcome measurement, and imprecision in effect estimate (Table 1).

sec.title: Mortality
sec.p: Three clinical trials analysed the effect of educational interventions on mortality, showing significant variability in the results (Greening et al., 2014; Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Silver et al., 2017). While the study by Silver et al. (2017) found no significant differences between the groups, positive effects were observed in the study by Linden and Butterworth (2014), both conducted in the United States. In this case, the multicomponent intervention included motivational interviewing-based health coaching, delivered by one of two experienced nurses trained in administering transitional care and motivational interviewing, showing a reduction in mortality among COPD patients at 90 days in the intervention group (p = 0.01; risk ratio [RR] = 0.28, 95 % CI 0.10-0.83; risk difference = –0.08, 95 % CI –0.15 to - 0.02) (Linden and Butterworth, 2014).

Contrarily, the clinical trial conducted by Greening et al. (2014) in the United Kingdom reported adverse effects from their educational intervention, which was based on physical exercises within a rehabilitation programme, confirming increased mortality in the intervention group at 12 months (OR 1.74, 95 % CI 1.05 to 2.88, p = 0.03).
According to the GRADE criteria, considering biases, limitations, and heterogeneity of results, the quality of the evidence was rated as very low (Table 1).

sec.title: Inhalation technique
sec.p: Three studies by the same author and centre (Press et al., 2020, 2016, 2012) analysed the number of errors committed by (asthma and COPD) patients using pressurised metered-dose inhalers (pMDIs) and Diskus® devices after an educational intervention. In 2012 and 2016, a brief in-person intervention was compared to a more intensive intervention (Teach To Goal, or TTG), while in 2020, a virtual intervention (V-TTG) was compared to the in-person TTG developed in 2012 and 2016. The follow-up effect was measured immediately after the intervention in all three studies; in 2016 and 2020, it was also measured at 30 and 90 days.

The TTG intervention reduced the misuse of pMDIs and Diskus® devices immediately after the intervention in 2012 and at 90 days in 2016 (p =0.005), but not at 30 days compared to a brief intervention (p = 0.06). However, no significant differences were observed between the virtual intervention and the TTG studied in 2020.

All three studies presented several risks of bias, mainly related to the study population, baseline differences between groups, and statistical analysis (only bivariate analysis was performed in 2012).
According to the GRADE system, the quality of evidence for the inhalation technique outcome was rated as low due to the aforementioned risks of bias and methodological limitations (Table 1).

sec.title: Skills and self-management
sec.p: Five randomized clinical trials analysed the effectiveness of various interventions on skills and self-management of the disease (Ho et al., 2025; Lara Romero et al., 2018; Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Press et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2021).

Three trials reported positive results immediately after and in the short term after discharge (Lara Romero et al., 2018; Press et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2021); however, in all cases, the statistical analysis was insufficient to establish a cause-effect relationship. Additionally, two of these studies had very small populations, making extrapolation impossible (Lara Romero et al., 2018; Press et al., 2012).

In the study by Linden and Butterworth (2014), the variables related to this outcome were not detailed in the methodology, nor were they included in the multiple analysis or the supplementary material.

Ho et al. (2025) included three items assessing participants’ ability to recall information. Both groups showed initial improvement at three months, followed by some decline between three and six months. However, when comparing the groups, participants in the intervention group were significantly more capable of recalling all selected information at both three and six months (p < 0.001). Nevertheless, the analysis was conducted solely using bivariate methods.
Considering these aspects, the biases, and the other domains analysed in GRADEpro, the quality of evidence for this outcome was rated as low (Table 1).

sec.title: Attitudes towards the disease
sec.p: Three studies examined this outcome in patients with COPD (Lara Romero et al., 2018; Linden and Butterworth, 2014; Press et al., 2012); additionally, Press et al. (2012) included patients with asthma, and Linden et al. (2014) included those with heart failure.

Only one of the studies reported results, showing an increase in the ‘description of strategies to prevent complications’ in the intervention group at discharge (p = 0.021) and at 30 days (p = 0.001). However, this study used an ad hoc, non-validated instrument to measure attitudes, had a very small sample size, and only conducted a bivariate analysis (Lara Romero et al., 2018).

In the studies by Press et al. (2012) and Linden and Butterworth (2014), attitudes were considered a secondary variable. Only the latter specified its measurement methodology but failed to explain the potential outcome values, providing only descriptive data and bivariate analysis in the baseline comparison, thus making it impossible to determine the intervention effects.

Given the data analysed, the study limitations, and the assessment of GRADE domains related to risk of bias and heterogeneity in intervention design and outcome measurement, the quality of evidence for this outcome was rated as (very low (Table 1).

sec.title: Adherence to inhaled therapy
sec.p: The variable was analysed by Lara et al. (2018) and Press et al. (2016) in patients with COPD.

In the first study, the Test of Adherence to Inhalers (TAI) was used to evaluate the results of an educational intervention during hospitalisation. The intergroup analysis found no statistically significant differences between the control and intervention groups at admission (X2 = 1.19; p = 0.75) or discharge (X2=2.88; p = 0.41). Conversely, the intragroup analysis showed a significantly higher proportion of adherent individuals at discharge compared to admission (McNemar = 16; p = 0.016), but no differences were detected in the control group at either time (McNemar = 14; p = 1.00). Evolution could not be analysed, as the one-month follow-up was conducted via telephone.

In the study by Press et al. (2016), adherence was measured with a subjective question about the number of missed doses, showing no differences in adherence to the Diskus® system between the intervention and control groups after discharge. However, no data were provided regarding the pMDI system.
The main limitations and risks of bias in both previously described studies relate to population size, sample characteristics, and data omission.
Therefore, the GRADE analysis indicates that the quality of evidence for the variable of adherence to inhaled therapy is very low (Table 1).

sec.title: Quality of life
sec.p: Four studies investigated this outcome (Benzo et al., 2016; Ho et al., 2025; Lopez-Lopez et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021), both involving patients with COPD and with follow-up periods at 6 and 12 months, and also at 3 months in the study by Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2021).

Benzo et al. (2016) conducted an intervention based on motivational interviewing (coaching accompanied by a written plan), measuring quality of life with the Chronic Respiratory Disease Questionnaire. They found a beneficial effect of the intervention in the domains of dyspnoea and fatigue in physical function at 6 months (NNT = 7; p = 0.038), and in emotional function at both 6 (NNT = 6; p = 0.018) and 12 months (NNT = 6; p = 0.036), as well as an increase in overall quality of life in the intervention group.

The intervention in the study by Wang et al. (2021) was based on the use of a health smartphone application, and they analysed health-related quality of life (HRQoL) using the COPD-Assessment Test (CAT). Significant differences were found between the intervention and control groups at 3 (p = 0.041), 6 (p = 0.003), and 12 months (p = 0.006).

Ho et al. (2025) implemented an intervention consisting of a pictorial action plan combined with a nurse-led educational session delivered prior to hospital discharge. This intervention provided structured information on daily medication use, exacerbation management, and appropriate responses to emergency situations. Health-related quality of life was assessed using the CAT, showing a significant improvement in the intervention group over time compared with the control group (B = 2.86; 95 % CI: −3.57 to −2.16; p < 0.001).

López-López et al. (2020) evaluated a multimodal intervention that combined an educational program focused on disease-related knowledge and healthy lifestyle habits with neuromuscular electrical stimulation therapy. Participants in the self-management group demonstrated significantly greater improvements in health-related quality of life at hospital discharge compared with the control and physiotherapy groups. At 3 month follow-up, these improvements were largely maintained in the self-management group, whereas partial deterioration was observed in the comparison groups.
The interventions and effect measures differed across studies. Additionally, neither study performed a multivariate analysis that would confirm the results.
Although positive long-term effects were demonstrated, the GRADE system indicates that the quality of evidence for the quality of life variable was rated as very low (Table 1).

sec.title: Exercise/physical activity
sec.p: Two clinical trials analysed the effects of different interventions on exercise/physical activity (Benzo et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2021). Benzo et al. (2016) conducted their study in the United States using a motivational interviewing-based health coaching intervention, plus a written action plan for exacerbations, and they found no effect on the variable studied. The primary limitation of their study was the lack of a description of this variable in the methodology, thus making it unclear how it was measured.

Conversely, the study by Wang et al. (2021) in China detailed the results of an intervention carried out through a mobile health application programme in addition to routine care, Their results confirmed an increase in the number of participants engaging in physical exercise more than three times per week in the intervention group at 3, 6, and 12 months, although there was no change in the duration of each exercise session. However, only bivariate analysis was provided.

After assessment using the GRADE system, the quality of evidence for the exercise/physical activity outcome was rated as very low due to risk of bias and heterogeneity in intervention design and outcome measurement (Table 1).

sec.title: Knowledge about the disease
sec.p: Lara et al. (2018) confirmed the benefit of the intervention in describing signs, symptoms, and strategies to prevent complications; however, the sample size was very small (n = 35) and only bivariate analyses were conducted.

In Linden et al., the same issues as for the outcomes of skills and self-management were observed: details were not provided in the methodology, multiple analysis, or supplementary material (Linden and Butterworth, 2014).

The analysis conducted according to the GRADE methodology, considering the small sample sizes, publication bias, and heterogeneity in intervention design and outcome measurement, suggests that the quality of evidence for this outcome is very low (Table 1).

sec.title: Length of hospital stay
sec.p: Greening et al. (2014) analysed patients with any chronic respiratory disease who underwent a rehabilitation exercise intervention during hospitalisation, finding no differences in the total number of hospital days between the control and intervention groups (difference 18 %, z =−0.5; p = 0.6).

Conversely, Silver et al. (2017) conducted an individualised educational intervention in COPD patients, accompanied by a written action plan. The results indicated that the conventional hospital stay duration in COPD patients following an exacerbation was shorter in the intervention group (306 days vs 523 days; p = 0.02), as was the stay for patients requiring ICU admission (306 days vs 523 days; p = 0.02).

Ho et al. (2025) also evaluated the effects of a pictorial action plan and observed a reduction in the mean length of hospital stay in the intervention group from 6.60 days (SD = 2.66) at T0 to 4.38 days (SD = 2.21) at T2, a decrease that was significantly greater than that observed in the control group (B = −1.20; 95 % CI: −1.88 to −0.51; p < 0.001).
The effect measures were only specified by Ho et al. (2025), whereas the other studies reported only bivariate data.
The GRADE analysis indicates that the quality of evidence for the outcome of hospital days is very low (Table 1).

sec.title: Smoking habit
sec.p: Smoking cessation was studied exclusively by Wang et al. (2021). They confirmed a decrease in the number of smokers and cigarettes per day in the intervention group, although the results were derived from bivariate analyses. Given the methodological limitations identified in this single study, the quality of evidence for this outcome was rated as very low according to the GRADE system (Table 1).

sec.title: Physical capacity
sec.p: Two studies investigated this outcome. Greening et al. (2014) observed patients with any chronic respiratory disease undergoing a rehabilitation programme during hospitalisation, with follow-up at discharge, at 6 weeks, and at 3 and 12 months. The results indicated an improvement in the shuttle walk test at six weeks (p = 0.02), but not in other functional measures recorded.

Lopez-Lopez et al. (2020) reported that all treatment groups improved functional capacity at hospital discharge; however, significantly greater gains were observed in the self-management group compared with the control and physiotherapy groups. Improvements were reflected in dyspnea-related functional limitation, general functionality, and lower-limb functional performance (p < 0.05). At three-month follow-up, functional outcomes deteriorated in the control and physiotherapy groups, whereas the self-management group showed relative maintenance of functional capacity, with persistent between-group differences favoring the intervention.

In addition, neither study reported effect measures, so the analyses were limited to bivariate comparisons. This limitation, together with other identified risks of bias—particularly those related to outcome reporting—indicates that the quality of evidence for the physical capacity outcome was rated as low according to the GRADE system (Table 1).

sec.title: Adherence to pharmacological treatment
sec.p: Only one study analysed these results (Lara Romero et al., 2018), finding negative effects after the intervention. However, it was conducted on a small sample and did not describe long-term follow-up due to being conducted by telephone, which was incompatible with the tool used in the initial in-person evaluation. Furthermore, the results were based on bivariate analysis, thus making it impossible to establish a cause-effect relationship.

According to the GRADE methodology, this single study presents serious limitations and risks of bias due to small sample size and issues related to outcome measurement; therefore, the quality of evidence for adherence to pharmacological treatment was rated as very low (Table 1).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review has analysed the effectiveness of educational interventions conducted during hospitalisation in patients with chronic respiratory disease, detailing both clinical and subjective outcomes such as quality of life and attitudes towards the disease, using the GRADE methodology. To the best of our knowledge, this is the only systematic review published to date that focuses on the application of interventions in the hospital setting, analysing 13 outcomes in total.

The overall certainty of evidence was rated as low or very low across all assessed outcomes. This classification was primarily driven by several methodological limitations observed in the included trials. Many studies raised concerns regarding random sequence generation or allocation procedures, and blinding was rarely feasible due to the nature of educational interventions, particularly affecting self-reported outcomes. In addition, most trials were based on small sample sizes, employed heterogeneous intervention designs, and applied non-standardised or study-specific outcome measures. These factors increased uncertainty and reduced comparability across studies. Taken together, these issues explain the downgrading of certainty in the GRADE assessment and highlight the need for more robust and methodologically rigorous trials.

Hospitalisation is frequently associated with suboptimal patient experiences, particularly among individuals with chronic respiratory disease. These experiences involve complex interdisciplinary interactions and the management of discharge planning and community follow-up. Improving patient education and awareness, enhancing professional training, ensuring continuity of care, and strengthening discharge processes are therefore essential. Achieving these goals requires patient-centred care environments, collaborative discharge planning, and the integration of home-based care to improve the overall quality of hospital services (An et al., 2025).

Exacerbations represent a considerable clinical issue for respiratory patients, particularly those with COPD (Hurst et al., 2020). This outcome was addressed in the majority of publications within this systematic review, being evaluated in eight studies, although only two specifically considered respiratory disease as the cause. Both studies confirmed the positive effect of multicomponent interventions in reducing COPD exacerbations. These findings are consistent with previous studies that suggest comprehensive multidisciplinary rehabilitation nursing interventions can reduce exacerbation frequency and improve lung function in COPD patients (Lenferink et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018; Zakrisson et al., 2011). Despite these encouraging results, the GRADE assessment indicates very low quality of evidence for this outcome, highlighting the need for cautious interpretation of these interventions’ effects, and suggesting the necessity of designing clinical trials that minimise bias and improve evidence quality.

Mortality is a crucial indicator for any healthcare system. Its high rates in Spain for both asthma and COPD (Gómez Sáenz et al., 2019; National Statistics Institute of Spain, 2024) underscore the importance of studying this outcome. In our review, mortality was assessed in two studies involving COPD patients, and in one study involving both COPD and asthma patients. The results were contradictory: positive in one study, negative in another, and no significant differences in the third, all indicating a very low quality of evidence for this outcome. These findings align with a recent Cochrane review, which concluded that self-care interventions for COPD patients do not significantly impact hospital admissions and mortality, regardless of the level of care or the patient’s phase (acute or stable) (Schrijver et al., 2022). In contrast, our review identified a positive relationship between interventions and quality of life. Nevertheless, due to insufficient statistical analysis and low-quality evidence, further research is required to identify the most effective activities for improving this vital, subjective aspect for respiratory patients (Hurst et al., 2020).

Another crucial health indicator is the length of hospital stay, analysed in only two studies within our review, with contradictory results. Comparison between studies is limited by substantial heterogeneity in intervention characteristics, which may explain the findings. Additionally, it is not possible to establish a statistical cause-effect relationship in either case.

Adherence to inhaled therapy is essential for effective management of respiratory diseases. Educational interventions focusing on inhalation technique are among the most relevant strategies for patients with asthma and COPD, frequently using structured methodologies such as Teach-To-Goal or Teach-Back to ensure skill acquisition and retention (Press et al., 2020, 2017). Adaptive digital educational interventions, such as personalised virtual modules, have also gained prominence. These enable the delivery of individualised sessions via digital platforms and have proven effective even when implemented in hospital settings (Press et al., 2020). In-person strategies include individualised educational sessions delivered during hospitalisation by trained clinical staff, supported by written or audiovisual materials (Kim et al., 2020; Press et al., 2017). These interventions may be supplemented by personalised action plans aimed at enhancing patient decision-making and preventing exacerbations (Vaquero-Lozano et al., 2021). Other modalities, such as post-discharge telephone follow-up or structured home-based educational visits, are also considered valuable strategies for promoting treatment adherence and reinforcing learning in the patient’s everyday environment (Kim et al., 2020). Despite differences in delivery mode, frequency, and intensity, they share a central focus: the development of key self-care competencies, in alignment with current international guidelines (Vázquez-González et al., 2023). The success of inhalation therapy depends on patients’ ability to correctly use the devices, and health education is crucial for achieving this. Education not only improves adherence but also enhances quality of life (Benito-Ruiz et al., 2024). The perception of the health education received to commence and continue treatment is well documented in the scientific literature, highlighting the need to increase the time dedicated to therapeutic education during healthcare visits and to address patients’ questions and concerns. Doctors and nurses are seen as the main providers of training and assessment in inhalation techniques (Dhand et al., 2018). However, findings from this review regarding inhalation technique and adherence were inconsistent and based on studies with small samples and substantial methodological limitations, resulting in low and very low-quality evidence. Future research should prioritise progressive, structured therapeutic education programmes incorporating in-person demonstrations, regular reassessment, and clear written recommendations (Benito-Ruiz et al., 2024).

Regarding outcomes related to disease knowledge, attitudes, skills, and self-management, our systematic review only provided relevant information on the latter two. The results were positive immediately after the intervention and in the short term after discharge, but with limited statistical power, making it impossible to establish a cause-effect relationship. The scant evidence found on these aspects is noteworthy, considering that a low level of disease knowledge negatively impacts the care and treatment of COPD patients (Uzel et al., 2017). It is important to recognise that healthcare professionals sometimes lack sufficient knowledge about the disease, necessitating adequate and systematic training in respiratory diseases and appropriate coordination to gain experience across different units and care levels, ultimately improving the knowledge of nurses and other professionals responsible for patient care (Ma et al., 2019).

Specific interventions aimed at improving exercise outcomes and physical capacity were also analysed in our review, demonstrating a very low quality of evidence due to the small number of publications, methodological biases, and the absence of relevant statistical analysis. The single study reporting improvements in physical capacity, despite these limitations, supports existing evidence on the benefits of rehabilitative and physical training for patients with COPD (Camillo et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2024), suggesting it could be a pertinent strategic direction for future research.

The inpatient environment also presents relevant logistical constraints, including short lengths of stay, high patient turnover, and the clinical workload of healthcare staff, all of which hinder longitudinal follow-up (Press et al., 2017). The evolution of health strategies and the existing scientific evidence support the multidisciplinary nature of care for chronic respiratory patients, highlighting the importance of coordination between different levels of care to prevent disease progression (Li et al., 2020). The implementation of TE programmes during hospitalisation has been shown to improve inhalation technique, increase patients’ perceived sense of control, and reduce inappropriate use of healthcare resources (Press et al., 2017; Trela et al., 2022).

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: Our study has several strengths. Firstly, a rigorous peer review was conducted, ensuring that all included articles were evaluated by all authors at some stage of the process. Secondly, the inclusion of the GRADE methodology to assess the quality of evidence for outcome variables adds significant value to the systematic review, providing internationally standardised, comparable, and reproducible values for other researchers.

However, several limitations must also be considered. Relevant studies published in languages other than English, Spanish, French, or Portuguese may have been missed, potentially limiting the comprehensiveness of the review. Additionally, some systematic reviews analysing the same topic do not distinguish between hospitalised patients and those in primary care, which may have led to the exclusion of potentially relevant evidence. A formal grey literature search was not conducted. Although the strategy focused on peer-reviewed studies indexed in major databases to ensure methodological consistency and editorial quality, this decision may have limited the comprehensiveness of the available evidence by excluding unpublished reports, theses, or conference proceedings. The findings should therefore be interpreted with consideration of the potential absence of relevant non-indexed studies. Moreover, several outcomes were examined in only one or two studies, often involving small sample sizes, providing only bivariate analyses, lacking meta-analytic approaches, or presenting substantial methodological bias, which limits the extrapolation of findings to other populations. The application of a predefined methodological quality threshold using the CASPe tool, while intended to prioritise internal validity, may have reduced the breadth of available evidence. Although this decision aimed to ensure minimum methodological standards, it may have restricted the scope of the included evidence and should be considered when interpreting the findings.

Furthermore, three of the included studies were conducted by the same research group, meaning that findings related to adherence and intervention effectiveness may be influenced by specific cultural and sociodemographic contexts (Press et al., 2020, 2016, 2012). Another relevant limitation is the variability in sex distribution across the included studies, particularly the underdiagnosis and underrepresentation of COPD in women, who were not included in one of the investigations (Ho et al., 2025). Recent evidence indicates that women experience greater exposure to certain risk factors, such as smoking, and present different comorbidity profiles, including anxiety, depression, malnutrition, and osteoporosis. These differences are associated with a higher frequency of exacerbations and complications, which negatively affect quality of life and disease progression. Taken together, these cultural and sociodemographic limitations warrant caution when extrapolating the results to other healthcare settings and populations (Borrego-Otero and Peña-Otero, 2025).

sec.title: Implications for research and practice
sec.p: Treating respiratory diseases is particularly challenging, as patients have complex social and health needs requiring lifelong monitoring and treatment. Nurses are in a strategic position to assume a leadership role in managing these conditions since they are often the first point of contact for patients, and are involved at all stages of care. Although evidence is still limited, an increasing number of studies suggest that nurse-led consultations and interventions have the potential to positively impact the health and quality of life of patients (Fletcher and Dahl, 2013).

There is also growing evidence supporting the role of innovative educational strategies in improving self-care effectiveness, and that nurse-led self-management programmes are effective in reducing hospital readmissions and emergency department visits (Zhou et al., 2025), as well as in improving exercise capacity and patient satisfaction among individuals with COPD (Wang et al., 2020).

It is also important to note that a primary goal of nurses is to address the needs of patients within both primary and hospital care settings, highlighting the hospitalisation period as a critical clinical phase where patients receive recommendations and new treatments (Rafferty and Elborn, 2004). From an epistemological nursing perspective, respiratory care extends beyond technical procedures (e.g., nebulisation, suctioning, oxygen therapy) to constitute a scientific and humanistic field focused on ventilation and oxygenation. This perspective aligns closely with theoretical self-care models, as it empowers patients to actively manage their respiratory health (Dorothea Orem, 2001).

The implementation of structured interventions during hospitalisation requires coordinated participation from multidisciplinary teams, including physiotherapists, physicians and pharmacists to achieve the best possible health outcomes for patients. Educational guidance should begin at the time of diagnosis and be reviewed at each encounter, both in primary care or hospital settings (Vaquero-Lozano et al., 2021).

Multimedia and digital interventions are gaining particular traction in the self-management of respiratory diseases and the administration of inhaled therapy, due to their accessibility, ease of use, and motivation for patients to remain adherent and aware of their chronic condition (Glynn et al., 2024). These include electronic monitoring devices or mobile applications as tools that support education in asthma or COPD, for instance.

Finally, future lines of research suggest broader application of data and artificial intelligence (AI), with demonstrated efficiency in recent studies (Mackenzie et al., 2024). AI-based systems enable the generation of personalised recommendations that can be delivered at key clinical moments to promote healthy lifestyle choices and early identification of problems, supporting healthy behaviours and early problem detection. AI-integrated decision support systems (DSS) can provide continuous improvement within a healthcare system, delivering the results of comprehensive analyses of large data sets with key information to users and professionals alike (Mackenzie et al., 2024).

sec.title: Funding sources
sec.p: This research was financially supported by the Spanish Society of Pulmonology and Thoracic Surgery (SEPAR) through a research grant (1419/2023) aimed at disseminating the results.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no new data were created or analyzed in this study.

Supplementary material: The document contains the complete Boolean search strings for each database, the data extraction of each study analyzed: citation, study (design, objectives, settings, professionals developing the intervention), PICO question, methods (participants per group, experimental and control group intervention, blinding and randomization, post-randomization losses, follow-up), results (positive and adverse effects, limitations) and conclusions.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Marta Sánchez-Zaballos: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yolanda Martín-Vaquero: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. David Díaz-Pérez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization. David Peña-Otero: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Marta Sanchez-Zaballos reports financial support was provided by Spanish Society of Pulmonology and Thoracic Surgery (SEPAR). If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ijnsa.2026.100520.
